home.title: demokrati
home.tagline: AI as lens, engine of influence, and object of governance

lens.title: Act I — AI as a democratic lens
lens.description: How AI can make politics, decisions, and conflicts more intelligible.

influence.title: Act II — AI as an engine of influence
influence.description: How AI reshapes political communication, framing, and opinion-making.

governance.title: Act III — AI as an object of governance
governance.description: How AI itself must be shaped by democratic principles.

home.intro: Seven demos about AI as democratic lens, engine of influence, and object of governance. Click any built demo — planned ones appear as placeholders with a short description.
home.disclaimer: These demos illustrate opportunities and risks for AI in the democratic information environment. They are not voting advisors, campaign tools, or automated decision support. All synthetic examples are labelled as synthetic.

sprakdriften.title: The Language Drift
sprakdriften.tagline: Same question, different languages — different answers
sprakdriften.intro: AI responses are not neutral information objects. They are linguistically and culturally situated. Språkdriften asks the same Swedish political question in several languages and shows what observably varies: tone, framing, institutions mentioned, risk posture. These are observed variations — not claims of measurable bias.
sprakdriften.waitingForResult: Waiting for the model. The first run can take up to a minute.
sprakdriften.method_toneExplainerBody: Tone is assigned by the comparison model as one of six labels: informative (neutral facts/context), analytical (weighs trade-offs), balanced (explicitly multi-sided), cautious (pervasively hedged), assertive (confident direct claims), advocating (takes a position). Heuristic, not measurement.
sprakdriften.method_certaintyExplainerBody: Certainty describes how confidently the text itself speaks — not the model's confidence in the content. High = mostly direct claims, few hedges. Medium = mix of claims and qualifiers. Low = pervasively hedged.
sprakdriften.dirDisclaimer: These are observed textual variations, not a scientific measure of bias. Tone and certainty labels are heuristic — see the methods panel.

riksdagsradarn.title: The Riksdag Radar
riksdagsradarn.tagline: What did parliament say about a topic, who said it, and how do the parties differ?
riksdagsradarn.intro: The Riksdag Radar searches actual speeches from the Swedish parliament, groups them by party, and summarises the debate. Every claim is linked back to the exact text segment so you can check what was actually said.
riksdagsradarn.waitingForResult: Retrieving relevant speeches, extracting claims, and building summaries. The first run takes about a minute.

regimeBlurbs.conflict: Foregrounds where parties disagree and who is criticising whom.
regimeBlurbs.citizen: What the debate means for an ordinary citizen, in plain language.

riksdagsradarn.method_claimTypeExplainerBody: Each claim is tagged as fact (empirical claim about how things are), value (normative claim about how things should be), critique (criticism of another actor or policy), or proposal (concrete recommendation or demand).
riksdagsradarn.method_confidenceExplainerBody: High = direct quote or near-paraphrase with multiple corroborating segments. Medium = one strong supporting segment. Low = inference beyond what the segments explicitly say.
riksdagsradarn.emptyResultBody: The search found no parliamentary speeches matching the topic and date range in the ingested corpus. Try a wider date range or different topic.
riksdagsradarn.dirDisclaimer: Summaries are AI-generated. Every claim is linked to source text — always verify against the original for sensitive interpretations. Source: the Riksdag of Sweden (open data).

oenighetskartan.title: The Disagreement Map
oenighetskartan.tagline: What is the disagreement actually about?
oenighetskartan.intro: This demo takes a curated corpus of statements on a policy question and asks a model to infer latent dimensions — fact, value, risk, trust, time horizon — that make the conflict legible. The axes are not hard-coded; they are inferred from this specific corpus.
oenighetskartan.waitingForResult: The model reads the corpus, identifies dimensions, and places each statement. Up to about a minute.
oenighetskartan.sameAxisHint: You have selected the same dimension on both axes — pick different dimensions to see the conflict structure.
oenighetskartan.notEnoughDimensions: The model did not find enough dimensions to draw a map.
oenighetskartan.clickAPoint: Click a point on the map to see the statement and its score on each dimension.
oenighetskartan.delib_sharedPremisesBlurb: Premises most statements in the corpus appear to accept.
oenighetskartan.delib_empiricalDisagreementsBlurb: Differences that could in principle be settled by evidence.
oenighetskartan.delib_valueConflictsBlurb: Differences that no amount of fact will resolve — they rest on different values.
oenighetskartan.delib_contestedTermsBlurb: Words used in different senses by different participants.
oenighetskartan.method_dimensionsExplainerBody: The model is asked to find 3–6 axes that separate the statements. They emerge from the current corpus, not a fixed list. Two different questions normally produce two different axis sets.
oenighetskartan.method_clustersExplainerBody: Statements are grouped into 2–4 thematic clusters by the model. Colours on the map correspond to clusters.
oenighetskartan.dirDisclaimer: This is an analytical hypothesis about the structure of the debate, not a psychological truth about the people who wrote the statements. Curated synthetic statements in v1.

aikonstitutionen.title: The AI Constitution
aikonstitutionen.tagline: The audience votes on rules — and the answers change.
aikonstitutionen.intro: AI governance is normative design as much as it is technology: neutrality, plurality, safety, intelligibility, and decisiveness must be weighed against each other. Here you ask a political question and pick rules an AI assistant must follow. We then compare an ungoverned and a governed answer.
aikonstitutionen.waitingForResult: The model produces an ungoverned answer, then a governed answer, then a comparison. About a minute.
aikonstitutionen.questionPlaceholder: e.g. Which party has the best climate policy?
aikonstitutionen.questionTooShort: The question must be at least 8 characters.
aikonstitutionen.constitutionBlurb: Toggle the rules you want the model to follow. Selected rules are compiled into a system prompt that governs the second answer.
aikonstitutionen.rulesApplied: {n, plural, =0 {no rules active} =1 {1 rule active} other {# rules active}}
aikonstitutionen.baselineBlurb: Default assistant with no audience-set rules.
aikonstitutionen.governedBlurb: Same question, under the chosen constitution.
aikonstitutionen.dirDisclaimer: The rules are an educational window onto normative governance — not a democratic regulation in itself. Trade-offs are heuristic, not measured.

oppenhetsparadoxen.title: The Openness Paradox
oppenhetsparadoxen.tagline: When the state is made intelligible — what changes and what is lost?
oppenhetsparadoxen.intro: Democratic accessibility requires translation. But every translation can shift meaning, tone, responsibility, or legal precision. Here we take an original text and produce five audience-tailored versions — and flag the meaning shifts.
oppenhetsparadoxen.waitingForResult: The model produces audience versions and analyses meaning shifts. About a minute.
oppenhetsparadoxen.method_diffsExplainerBody: The model flags concrete textual shifts: dropped conditions/exceptions, changed actor or modality (must→should), simplified thresholds, added emphasis, or lost specificity. This is text analysis — not a legal opinion.
oppenhetsparadoxen.dirDisclaimer: This is text analysis, not a legal review. The model does not claim legal expertise.

persuasionmaskinen.title: The Persuasion Engine
persuasionmaskinen.tagline: Personalised political influence at scale — cheaper and more invisible with AI.
persuasionmaskinen.intro: This demo shows how the same political question can be framed differently for different voter profiles. The threat is not just disinformation — it is that the public sphere fragments: different voters see different arguments, tones, and descriptions of reality.
persuasionmaskinen.ethicsBody: All profiles are synthetic. All policy cases are fictional. No real parties, candidates, or voter data are used. The demo shows the mechanism — it is not a campaign tool and must not be used as one.
persuasionmaskinen.waitingForResult: The model produces a generic message and four tailored versions. 30–60 seconds.
persuasionmaskinen.dirDisclaimer: Profiles and cases are synthetic. The demo exposes the mechanism so we can think about why democratic infrastructure (transparency, ad traceability, platform accountability) needs updating.

programkompassen.title: The Programme Compass
programkompassen.tagline: How party programmes translate into positions on concrete policy questions.
programkompassen.intro: This demo retrieves passages from each party's own programme text and asks a model to extract a 1–5 stance on each question. Not a voting advisor — a review of how fixed ideology translates into selectable positions.
programkompassen.waitingForResult: The model searches party programme texts and extracts positions. 1–2 minutes depending on party count.
programkompassen.method_stanceExplainerBody: Stance 1–5 where 3 = unclear/mixed. Confidence describes how direct the support is: high = direct quote, low = inference. Stance 3 + low confidence = the party does not address the question in indexed text.
programkompassen.dirDisclaimer: The demo is not a voting advisor. It compares party programme texts against the questions — parties may hold positions that do not appear in the programme text or that have changed since the programme was written. Source: SND collection 'Vi vill...' and direct from party websites.
